region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Punjabi); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for butter paneer; mapped to the parent class 'curry dish' (FOODON_03311737) as the closest defensible ancestor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of butter paneer is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a rich tomato-cream curry with cubed paneer, served alongside naan or steamed basmati rice.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Indian curry composite reference; cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for paneer, butter, cream, and tomato-based curry sauce
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for butter paneer as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighted-ingredient estimation using ICMR-NIN paneer data (protein ~18 g/100 g, fat ~20 g/100 g) and USDA SR Legacy entries for butter, cream, tomato purée, and cashew, blended to reflect a typical restaurant-style butter paneer formulation (~35% paneer, ~15% cream/butter, ~40% tomato-onion sauce, ~10% cashew paste). Input record values (energy 187.5 kcal, protein 7.5 g, fat 13.75 g, carbohydrate 8.75 g) are consistent with this estimation and are retained. Trans fat 0.38 g/100 g is plausible from dairy-sourced butter. Sodium 300 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant salt addition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed Indian curry with paneer and tomato-cream sauce
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for butter paneer in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on: (1) paneer has negligible GI contribution as a high-protein, high-fat dairy food; (2) tomato-cream sauce base has low GI (~35); (3) cashew paste contributes moderate GI (~25); (4) overall dish carbohydrate content is modest at 8.75 g/100 g with significant fat and protein blunting. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 8.75 × 3) / 100 = 11.8 g. This is classified as low GL (<11 is low; 11–19 is medium — borderline low-medium). Classified as low GI. Analogous reference: mixed vegetable curry with dairy, GI ~40–50, University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; elevated saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g from butter and cream
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt and cream; the tomato and onion base provides potassium (~200 mg/100 g estimated) which partially offsets sodium, but saturated fat from butter and cream remains a secondary cardiovascular concern that warrants moderation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 8.75 g/100 g, low sugar 2.5 g/100 g, high protein 7.5 g/100 g from paneer slows gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Butter paneer has a low glycemic index (~45) and modest carbohydrate load, making it suitable for people with type 2 diabetes when consumed in a 150–200 g portion paired with wholemeal roti rather than white naan; kasoori methi (fenugreek) and turmeric in the spice blend have evidence-based hypoglycaemic and insulin-sensitising properties, but the high saturated fat content from butter and cream should be monitored to avoid worsening insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g and trans fat 0.38 g/100 g from butter and cream; cholesterol 30 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Butter paneer is high in saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) and contains dairy-derived trans fat (0.38 g/100 g), both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portions to 100–150 g, request substitution of butter with extra-virgin olive oil and reduction of cream, and pair the dish with high-fibre accompaniments to mitigate the atherogenic fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; tomatoes are nightshades
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Butter paneer is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat at 7.5 g/100 g (pro-inflammatory, exceeds RA threshold) and the tomato-based sauce (nightshade); however, the anti-inflammatory spice blend of turmeric, ginger, garlic, and fenugreek is beneficial, and the dish could approach suitability if butter is replaced with EVOO, cream is reduced, and nightshade sensitivity is absent.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Paneer (also known as Paneer Makhani or Paneer Butter Masala) is a classic North Indian Punjabi curry dish consisting of cubed paneer — a fresh, non-melting Indian cottage cheese — simmered in a rich, velvety sauce made from butter, heavy cream, tomato purée, onion, garlic, ginger, and cashew paste, seasoned with garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and kasoori methi (dried fenugreek leaves). The dish is characterised by its deep orange-red colour, creamy texture, and a mildly spiced, slightly tangy flavour profile balanced by the sweetness of cream and the earthiness of fenugreek. It is one of the most popular Indian vegetarian dishes globally and is widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, butter paneer provides approximately 187.5 kcal per 100 g, with 7.5 g protein from paneer, 13.75 g fat (predominantly saturated at 7.5 g from butter and cream), and 8.75 g carbohydrate. Calcium content is notable at 100 mg/100 g from paneer. The dish has a low glycemic index (~45) due to its high protein and fat content blunting glucose absorption, making it relatively suitable for type 2 diabetes management in controlled portions. However, the high saturated fat content raises concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The anti-inflammatory spice blend — particularly turmeric (curcumin), ginger, garlic, and fenugreek — provides clinically relevant phytochemical benefits. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 300 g, typically accompanied by naan or basmati rice. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for butter paneer as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are derived from weighted-ingredient estimation using ICMR-NIN paneer data and USDA SR Legacy entries for butter, cream, tomato, and cashew. Input record values are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-established and the estimation methodology is transparent and cross-validated.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for butter paneer as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level MUFA/PUFA data exists but reliable blended values for the dish as prepared cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium for the composite dish is not directly reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation; estimated ~200 mg/100 g from tomatoes, onions, and cashews but not confirmed by a single approved-source measurement. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for butter paneer as a composite restaurant dish are not available as a single validated entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central; individual ingredient data exists but blended composite values are not confirmed. 4. Input record described the dish as 'Mexican' cuisine — this is incorrect; butter paneer is a North Indian (Punjabi) dish. Cuisine corrected to Indian. Cultural tags 'Mexican', 'Taco', 'Burrito' removed as unsupported. 5. Input record cr_id G00011945 and name 'BUTTER PANNER' (misspelling of paneer) preserved in metadata; canonical name corrected to 'Butter Paneer'. 6. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is widely served in Singapore. 7. Trans fat 0.38 g/100 g is plausible from dairy-derived butter and is retained from input. 8. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for butter paneer.
